title: The Sonnets XVIII - Shall I compare thee to a summerï؟½s day?
content:
آ آ آ آ Shall I compare thee to a summerï؟½s day?
آ آ آ آ Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
آ آ آ آ Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,
آ آ آ آ And summerï؟½s lease hath all too short a date:
آ آ آ آ Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
آ آ آ آ And often is his gold complexion dimmï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And every fair from fair sometime declines,
آ آ آ آ By chance, or natureï؟½s changing course untrimmï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ But thy eternal summer shall not fade,
آ آ آ آ Nor lose possession of that fair thou owï؟½st,
آ آ آ آ Nor shall death brag thou wanderï؟½st in his shade,
آ آ آ آ When in eternal lines to time thou growï؟½st,
آ آ آ آ So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
آ آ آ آ So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Shakespeare, titled "The Sonnets XVIII - Shall I compare thee to a summerï؟½s day?", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...
